---
title: "Devices and analog circuits"
description: "From semiconductor behavior to measured analog signal chains."
page-role: part
part-id: analog
---

Part I treated sources, loads, and passive networks. Part II introduces
components whose terminal behavior changes with operating point, temperature,
frequency, and fabrication. You will use those devices to switch, amplify,
condition, and sense real signals.

## The part question

> How can semiconductor devices shape and amplify signals while staying within
> their electrical, thermal, noise, bandwidth, linearity, and stability limits?

A useful answer links material behavior to terminal curves, circuit operation,
datasheet limits, physical implementation, and test results. The connection
matters because an active circuit can produce the expected nominal output while
still violating a rating or losing margin at another operating condition.

## Entry knowledge

Part II assumes that you can:

- reduce a source–load network and find a DC operating point using F03–F04;
- account for power, stored energy, temperature, and ratings using F05 and F07;
- plan a measurement and include instrument loading and uncertainty using F06;
- use impedance, phasors, transfer functions, and frequency response from F08.

[M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd) supports logarithms, decibels,
complex impedance, and transfer functions.
[T01](../appendices/t01-datasheets-standards.qmd) supports device selection, and
M03 provides optional support for the calibration and statistical-variation work
in A05–A07.

Practical work remains current limited and extra low voltage unless an approved
procedure establishes another boundary. The
[preface](../preface.qmd) states the book-wide safety and evidence conventions.

## The learning arc

| Chapter | Main question | Capability |
|---|---|---|
| [A01](a01-semiconductor-diodes.qmd) | How does semiconductor structure produce direction- and voltage-dependent conduction? | relate junction physics to terminal curves and design diode circuits within device limits |
| [A02](a02-transistors.qmd) | How can a small electrical signal control a larger current or voltage? | operate BJTs and MOSFETs as switches or controlled sources and check drive, loss, and thermal margin |
| [A03](a03-bias-small-signal-amplifiers.qmd) | How can a nonlinear transistor amplify a small changing signal? | establish bias and predict incremental gain, loading, headroom, bandwidth, and distortion |
| [A04](a04-op-amps-feedback.qmd) | When does feedback make amplifier behavior predictable? | design common op-amp functions and check range, offset, slew, bandwidth, recovery, and stability |
| [A05](a05-sensors-instrumentation.qmd) | How does a physical quantity become a calibrated electrical result? | design the path from sensor and excitation through conditioning, calibration, and uncertainty |
| [A06](a06-noise-nonlinearity-stability.qmd) | Which non-ideal effect limits a real analog chain? | refer errors to a common point and assess noise, distortion, dynamic range, bandwidth, and stability |
| [A07](a07-integrated-circuits.qmd) | What changes when transistors and interconnect share one piece of silicon? | analyze CMOS building blocks and account for matching, variation, parasitics, layout, package, and test |
| [A08](a08-optoelectronics-hmi.qmd) | How can a system convert between electricity and light and present state clearly? | design optical interfaces and indicators for their electrical and human operating conditions |

The progression starts with device behavior in A01–A02. A03–A04 then turn
transistor gain into controlled gain. A05–A06 apply that gain to measurement and
quantify the effects that limit a signal chain. A07 moves from schematic devices
to integrated implementation, while A08 follows the optical and human-facing
branch.

## Scope boundaries

Some topics appear in several chapters because each chapter asks a different
question:

- A01 introduces LED and photodiode junction behavior. A08 owns complete optical
  interfaces, displays, and human-readable indication.
- A03 introduces noise, bandwidth, and distortion in a transistor stage. A04
  introduces feedback stability. A06 owns chain-level performance budgets.
- A07 treats the CMOS inverter as an integrated transistor circuit. D01 starts
  from electrical ranges and develops logic values.
- A05 ends with a conditioned analog quantity. S02 owns sampling, quantization,
  and converter architecture.
- A02 introduces low-energy switching. S05 adds converter topology, magnetics,
  control, and the hazards of a power stage.

## Prerequisite structure

Each arrow in @fig-analog-dependencies represents a direct prerequisite in
[`chapters.toml`](../chapters.toml).

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-analog-dependencies
%%| fig-cap: "Direct prerequisites for Part II. Page order and conceptual handoffs are described in the text."
%%| fig-alt: "F04 and F05 lead to A01. A01 and F05 lead to A02. A02 and F08 lead to A03. A03 and F08 lead to A04. A04 and F06 lead to A05. A04 and F08 lead to A06. A02 and A03 lead to A07. A01 and A05 lead to A08."
%%| fig-width: 6.1
flowchart TB
  F04["F04<br/>network equivalents"]
  F05["F05<br/>power and heat"]
  F06["F06<br/>measurement"]
  F08["F08<br/>frequency response"]

  A01["A01<br/>diodes"]
  A02["A02<br/>transistors"]
  A03["A03<br/>bias and gain"]
  A04["A04<br/>op-amps and feedback"]
  A05["A05<br/>instrumentation"]
  A06["A06<br/>analog limits"]
  A07["A07<br/>integrated circuits"]
  A08["A08<br/>optoelectronics and HMI"]

  F04 --> A01
  F05 --> A01
  F05 --> A02
  A01 --> A02
  F08 --> A03
  A02 --> A03
  F08 --> A04
  A03 --> A04
  F06 --> A05
  A04 --> A05
  F08 --> A06
  A04 --> A06
  A02 --> A07
  A03 --> A07
  A01 --> A08
  A05 --> A08
```

The main active-device route is A01 → A02 → A03 → A04. From there, A05 and A06
develop measurement and performance analysis in parallel. You can enter A07
after A02–A03. A08 combines the junction behavior from A01 with the measurement
chain from A05.

## Practical work

| Lab | Aligned chapters | What you demonstrate |
|---|---|---|
| **L06** Diode and transistor operating regions | A01, A02 | connect measured terminal curves and switching waveforms to operating region and device limits |
| **L07** Amplifier gain, limits, and feedback | A03, A04, A06 | compare bias, gain, frequency response, clipping, noise, and stability with circuit predictions |
| **L08** Sensor calibration and uncertainty | A05, F06, R05 | carry a defined measurand through conditioning and calibration to a stated decision |

Milestone **M2 — build a calibrated environmental monitor** uses device
protection, switching, sensing, and presentation. **M3 — create a precision
instrument** combines A03–A06 with sampling and metrology. A07 uses a different
evidence path: transistor-level analysis across process, voltage, and
temperature, followed by layout-aware parasitic comparison. That analysis does
not constitute measurement of fabricated hardware.

The [practice and project spine](../roadmap.qmd#practice-and-project-spine)
provides the full integration map.

## Exit capability

After Part II, you should be able to:

- choose a diode, BJT, MOSFET, op-amp, or sensor approximation for stated
  operating conditions;
- establish bias and headroom and identify cutoff, saturation, clipping, slew
  limitation, instability, and thermal failure;
- design feedback circuits while checking their range, finite gain, bandwidth,
  recovery, and stability;
- construct a calibrated measurement chain with protection, grounding or
  isolation, and an uncertainty budget;
- identify which noise, distortion, interference, or bandwidth term limits the
  chain;
- explain how process, geometry, matching, parasitics, layout, package, and test
  alter an integrated block;
- design an optical interface or indicator for its electrical, ambient, power,
  readability, and accessibility requirements.

These capabilities support digital logic, data conversion, feedback control,
power electronics, PCB realization, metrology, and complete instruments.

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter maturity"}
A01–A08 are teaching drafts. The
[reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd#interpret-chapter-status) explains what each
status supports.
:::
